data.title: Davenport Equity Opportunities Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Q4 2025 Market Review

The Davenport Equity Opportunities Fund (DEOPX) declined 4.07% during the fourth quarter, finishing the year down 2.61%. This compared to the Russell Mid Cap® Index, which experienced gains of 0.16% and 10.60% for the quarter and year, respectively. This was certainly a difficult close to a frustrating year. While we had several standout performers throughout the year, their returns were muted by a handful of key detractors. This made it difficult to keep up with the index, which received additional thrust from AI momentum darlings that continued their ascent in spite of stratospheric valuations. While frustrated, we have tremendous faith in our process and continue to execute against it with rigorous discipline. We expect this commitment to quality, cash flow, capital allocation and valuation to pay off over time.

Fund Update

Contributors: Align Technology Inc. (ALGN) was the strategy’s top performer during the period, bouncing nearly 25% from depressed levels alongside stabilizing results and more constructive price commentary. Other top contributors included Markel Corp (MKL), Etsy Inc. (ETSY) and new addition Cooper Companies Inc. (COO). We elected to exit our position in Etsy with the stock on a spike. We provide more detail on our purchase of Cooper below.

Detractors: Key detractors included Alexandra Real Estate Equities Inc. (ARE), Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY), Generac Holdings Inc. (GNRC) and Watsco Inc. (WSO). In the case of ARE, we had begun to build a position into the company’s most recent earnings report; however, abandoned the effort following deterioration in business trends which resulted in a dramatic change in the company’s cash flow outlook. On the other hand, we added to positions in Watsco and Generac amid weakness.

Fund Activity

We initiated a position in Cooper Companies during the quarter. Cooper is the world’s second-largest player in the contact lens market, just behind Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and slightly ahead of Alcon AG (ALC). We have long admired the company’s annuity-like business model, as contact lens wearers require regular replacements and category growth is also supported by the natural decline in visual acuity with age. We further respect Cooper’s reputation as an R&D leader, underscored by its pioneering products, including the only FDA-approved pediatric contact lens for myopia management and a joint venture for pediatric spectacle lenses. Specialty lenses for astigmatism and multi-focal needs, along with a shift toward daily disposable lenses, should continue to support premium pricing and strong revenue growth. With the stock experiencing a significant decline following a disappointing quarterly earnings report, we were able to initiate a position with the shares cheaper than they have been in over a decade. After our purchase, we were pleased to see several meaningful insider purchases alongside the announcement of a strategic review process that could unlock value from the sale on non-core operations.

We also initiated a position in Carrier Global Corp (CARR), largest provider of heating, ventilation and air- conditioning (HVAC) equipment and services for the US residential market. Long term, we note the company is well positioned to benefit from secular growth drivers such as decarbonization, energy efficiency mandates, and heat pump adoption. Further, the company’s expanding services and aftermarket business provides a significant runway for margin improvement. Despite short-term headwinds in US residential HVAC, Carrier’s commercial and international segments are seeing healthy growth, and the company is also well positioned to benefit from macro catalysts like a housing recovery and increased infrastructure spending. With the stock trading at a significant discount to peers and historical averages, we thought the shares timely. Finally, we note that we are in good company with this belief given that CEO Dave Gitlin also purchased ~$1.0 million of stock at similar levels.

Conclusion

In sum, we are thankful for the opportunity to leave a disappointing year in the rearview mirror and remain enthusiastic about the strategy’s positioning headed into 2026. Though we made some mistakes and found ourselves on the wrong side of the momentum trade in 2025, we continued to apply our disciplined approach to emphasizing quality and optimizing around risk/reward. We are excited about the new businesses we are able to add to the fold during the year and feel the strategy is differentiated and timely at current levels.

Sincerely,

George L. Smith III, CFA®

Christopher G. Pearson, CFA®

data.title: Dodge & Cox Balanced Fund Q4 2025 Shareholder Letter
data.description:
Market Commentary

Financial markets delivered another strong year in 2025, though not without turbulence from tariff uncertainty, a record 43-day government shutdown, and a cooling labor market. The S&P 500 Index rose 18%, notching its third consecutive year of double-digit gains. Artificial intelligence remained a dominant theme, and Information Technology and Communication Services were the top-contributing sectors. Market concentration stayed elevated, and the ten largest companies contributed more than half of the Index’s gains. Overseas, international equities beat U.S. stocks by roughly 15 percentage points—a historically wide margin—and emerging markets had their strongest showing in nearly a decade.

Bonds had a solid year as well. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index returned over 7%, marking its best year since 2020, as interest rates declined and corporate and mortgage-backed bonds outperformed.

The U.S. economy remained resilient with third-quarter GDP growing at a brisk 4.3% annualized rate. But cracks emerged: the unemployment rate climbed to a four-year high of 4.6% by November. As inflation continued to moderate, the Federal Reserve responded with three rate cuts throughout the year, including two in the fourth quarter, bringing cumulative easing to 175 basis points since September 2024. With rates now closer to neutral, Fed Chair Powell signaled the Fed can be patient before making its next move.

Mutual Fund performance changes over time and currently may be significantly lower than stated above. Performance is updated and published monthly. Current month-end performance can be obtained at dodgeandcox.com or by calling 800-621-3979.

Portfolio Strategy

Broad U.S. equity valuations look stretched by historical standards, suggesting more modest future returns. In fixed income, real yields are attractive, but tight credit spreads call for discipline. Against this backdrop, the Fund’s asset allocation remained relatively steady, but this stability masks meaningful activity at the security level. As of December 31, the Fund held 48% in U.S. equities, 18% in non-U.S. equities, and 34% in fixed income securities plus cash.2

In 2025, we initiated 13 new equity positions in companies where we believe long-term fundamentals are not reflected in current prices. New holdings span semiconductor equipment, insurance brokers, food products, and software. We also built a position in the Dodge & Cox Emerging Markets Stock Fund, which provides efficient exposure to a diverse group of emerging market companies trading at appealing valuations.3 At the same time, we trimmed or exited positions where valuations became less compelling. As a result, we believe the equity portfolio is more reasonably priced and diversified than the broader market.

Within fixed income, we shifted the mix—reducing the portfolio's Credit sector exposure (now near multi-year lows) and adding to Securitized holdings (near multi-year highs). With credit spreads near their tightest levels in decades, we favor high-quality securitized assets over chasing yield in expensive corporate credit. The Fund’s Securitized allocation centers on Agency4 mortgage-backed securities, which offer solid return potential across rate environments and high liquidity.

Outlook

We’re optimistic about the Fund’s long-term return prospects. The combination of attractively valued equities and a diversified bond portfolio positions the Fund well for a range of outcomes—and we're prepared to act when volatility creates opportunity. Thank you for your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.

Performance Review (Fund’s Class I Shares) Fourth Quarter

Equity security selection drove the Fund’s relative underperformance. This was partially offset by positive equity sector allocation.

Equity Portfolio (vs. S&P 500)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Industrials stock selection, including FedEx (FDX);
  • Stock selection and underweight position in Information Technology, particularly underweight position in Microsoft (MSFT);
  • Overweight position in Health Care, primarily Regeneron Pharmaceuticals (REGN) and GSK (GSK); and
  • Underweight position in Meta Platforms (META).

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Financials stock selection, mainly Fiserv (FISV);
  • Energy holdings, notably Occidental Petroleum (OXY);
  • Communication Services stock selection, including Charter Communications (CHTR) and underweight position in Alphabet (GOOG); and holding in Alibaba (BABA).

Fixed Income Portfolio (vs. Bloomberg U.S. Agg)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Security selection within Agency MBS and overweight to the sector;
  • Underweight to U.S. Treasuries; and
  • Credit security selection, notably British American Tobacco and Oracle.

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio’s:

  • Position in Charter Communications.

2025

Strong security selection within equities was the main driver of the Fund’s relative outperformance. Fixed income security selection and sector allocation also benefited relative results. These factors were partially offset by weak equity sector allocation.

Equity Portfolio (vs. S&P 500)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Consumer Discretionary stock selection and underweight position;
  • Health Care stock selection, mainly CVS Health (CVS) and GSK;
  • Industrials holdings, notably RTX (RTX);
  • Consumer Staples stock selection and underweight position; and
  • Holding in Banco Santander (SAN).

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio’s:

  • Underweight position in Information Technology;
  • Materials stock selection and overweight position;
  • Communication Services holdings and underweight position, particularly Charter Communications; and
  • Positions in Fiserv, Occidental Petroleum, and Avantor (AVTR).

Fixed Income Portfolio (vs. Bloomberg U.S. Agg)

Key contributors to relative results included the portfolio's:

  • Credit security selection, particularly Pemex as well as British American Tobacco, Citigroup, and Republic of Colombia;
  • Security selection within Agency MBS and overweight to the sector; and
  • Underweight to U.S. Treasuries and overweight to corporate bonds.

Key detractors from relative results included the portfolio’s:

  • Below-benchmark duration position.

data.title: A Near-Perfect 9% Yielding Dividend Growth Machine For Retirement
data.description:
My ideal investment to retire on passive income is a high-quality, durable growth business backed by a strong investment-grade balance sheet that pays out a very high yield that is not only sustainable but likely to grow at a rate that meets or beats inflation for the foreseeable future. While such opportunities are rare because the market tends to value such securities highly and thereby price them up to a point where their yield falls to a lower level, there are some attractive opportunities still out there that meet these requirements, and today's article will detail two of them.

Opportunity #1

The first one I want to talk about is Plains All American Pipeline (PAA), which is a K-1 issuing MLP (AMLP) that is primarily focused on crude oil (USO) related businesses. In particular, it's focused on the Permian Basin, which still has assets that extend all the way up to Canada.

The company has a strong balance sheet with a 3.3x leverage ratio that is on the low end of its 3.25x to 3.75x target range and gives it a solid investment-grade credit rating. It also has well-laddered debt maturities, with $750 million of debt maturing this year, but then no debt maturing until 2029 and no more than $1 billion of debt maturing any single year until 2036. It even has debt that is laddered all the way out until the 2040s, which gives it significant financial flexibility, especially since the amount of debt maturing this year is only about 7.5% of its total outstanding long-term debt.

The company also has a highly contracted cash flow profile, with 85% of adjusted EBITDA on a pro forma basis from its NGL business divestiture being commodity price resistant either through minimum volume commitment contracts, acreage dedications, or durable movement contracts. With a weighted average contract term of about five years, it gives investors an opportunity to make a concentrated bet on oil production in the Permian Basin while hedging commodity price volatility over the near to medium term.

This is not a bad bet, given that numerous agencies expect global oil demand to remain pretty stable over the next 25 years, and in particular see the Permian Basin as being a major contributor to global oil supply in the coming years.

In addition to its strong balance sheet and durable and defensive business model, PAA has a very attractive value proposition, especially for income-focused investors, as it has a 9.1% next 12-month distribution yield, and analysts expect it to grow its distribution at a 6% CAGR through the end of the decade. Meanwhile, its distributable cash flow per unit is expected to grow at a 3.7% CAGR over that same time period.

This means it should pay attractive current income and grow it at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time. On top of that, PAA issues a K-1 tax form, which provides some tax benefits to unit holders who hold it in a taxable account, as distributions are typically classified as return of capital. Meanwhile, for investors who want to avoid the K-1, it issues an economic equivalent, Plains GP Holdings (PAGP).

PAA's distribution is well covered as well, as, in addition to the strong balance sheet and stable cash flow profile, the projection for the 2026 distribution is that it will be covered 1.55x by distributable cash flow, which provides a substantial buffer for the distribution and makes it quite sustainable for the foreseeable future.

Opportunity #2

The other high-yielding opportunity that I'm going to talk about today is Western Midstream Partners (WES). Like PAA, it is also an MLP that issues a K-1 tax form, though unlike PAA, it does not have an economic equivalent that issues a 1099 tax form. It also pays out a 9.1% next twelve-month distribution yield, which is very attractive and is expected to grow that distribution at a solid, albeit less impressive, 3.5% CAGR over the next several years.

That being said, its distributable cash flow per unit growth profile is much more attractive at a 10.2% CAGR over that same time frame, as management is trying to boost the distributable cash flow coverage ratio of its distribution to make it more sustainable over time, even if the company were to hit a speed bump.

Another big advantage that WES has relative to PAA is that it has more diversified commodity price exposure, with a water business that is growing rapidly, thanks to the company's recent acquisition of Aris Water Solutions, and also has a natural gas (UNG) business on top of its crude oil and NGLs. The company also has high investment-grade counterparty exposure, with 79% of its revenue coming from investment-grade counterparties, and like PAA, its cash flows are highly contracted.

In fact, its gas contracts are 95% fee-based, and its liquids contracts are 100% fee-based. Meanwhile, its gas contracts have a nine-year weighted average remaining life, its oil contracts have a seven-year weighted average remaining life, and its water contracts have an eight-year weighted average remaining life.

Some of these contracts were further lengthened by a recent modification of some of the cost-of-service contracts with Occidental Petroleum (OXY) that created a more stable fee-based structure and extended their duration to well into the 2030s (while likely reducing the profitability of these contracts over time) in exchange for the cancellation of a significant number of WES units that were previously held by OXY.

WES also has a very strong balance sheet, recently obtained an investment-grade credit rating, and has over $2 billion worth of liquidity, with $441 million of debt maturing this year and the remainder of its debt maturing in 2028 and beyond. The WES leverage ratio is quite low at just 2.8x, giving it significant flexibility to add incremental leverage if needed to invest in attractive growth opportunities or repurchase units.

WES plans to continue investing in opportunistic bolt-on acquisitions as well as organic growth investments to continue driving growth that is above a low-to-mid single-digit distribution CAGR for the foreseeable future in order to further enhance its distribution safety with an over 9% current yield, which sets WES to provide very attractive risk-adjusted income, total returns, and inflation protection on the purchasing power of that income.

Risks and Investor Takeaway

While both PAA and WES are likely going to be sensitive to commodity prices in terms of their unit price performance in the short term, their cash flows are actually quite resistant to commodity price volatility in the near term. Additionally, PAA is particularly vulnerable given a focus on a single geographic region and commodity, whereas WES also has some geographic concentration risk, especially relative to the better diversified midstream peers like Enbridge (ENB), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and Energy Transfer (ET). However, at least it has diversification across natural gas, oil, NGLs, and water.

Both businesses also have some project execution and bolt-on acquisition risk, which could lead to misallocating capital over time. However, their recent track record has shown that they are exercising pretty good discipline in that area, and they've carved out pretty competitive positionings that enhance the odds that they will return to attractive returns on capital.

As a result, these are two very large positions in my 7% to 8% yielding, total return outperforming, and low beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: CSQ: Tax-Efficient Dividends That Can Continue To Grow
data.description:
Overview

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund (CSQ) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns from a blending portfolio of equities and fixed income securities. This blend allows the fund to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation and a high rate of income generation. When I previously covered CSQ, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. Since my last coverage, CSQ has released an updated annual report, which prompted me to revisit the fund's performance and value proposition now that we've crossed into the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CSQ's share price has increased by 4.7%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to partially participate in the positive market momentum of the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 12.3% over the same time frame. CSQ now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.6% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. After reviewing the recent reporting, the fund has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that far exceed the distributions being paid. CSQ continues to be an excellent choice for investors seeking income stability and growth over time.

Despite market indices trading to new all-time highs, CSQ's valuation remains extremely attractive. The fund has delivered healthy NAV growth over the last year, but the price to NAV discount has increased since the time of my last coverage. This can be an indication that there's a price disconnect and long-term investors may take advantage of this weakness by accumulating. However, there are some structural flaws within the fund's portfolio strategy that should be acknowledged. For instance, the fund's net investment income has struggled to remain consistent in this higher interest rate environment, which means that CSQ may not offer an efficient level of resilience during market declines. So let's start by reviewing the fund's underlying strategy that's implemented to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, CSQ has total managed assets of $4.6B that are spread across a blended portfolio of assets. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 2.24%, but this is a bit misleading because it includes fees related to interest expenses and other operating costs. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 1% of total assets. The fund aims to provide attractive total returns by getting a combination of capital appreciation and income generation.

One of the main focuses of the fund is that management wants to prioritize a distribution that they believe to be sustainable. So unlike other high yield closed-end funds, CSQ's distributions will remain consistent through different interest rate environments. In order to achieve this goal, the fund implements a blend between common stocks and other income producing securities. The fund's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Common Stock: 67.2%
  • Convertibles: 16.3%
  • Corporate Bonds: 8.4%
  • Bank Loans: 2.7%
  • Other: 2.7%
  • Cash and Receivables/Payables: 2.2%
  • Synthetic Convertibles: 0.2%
  • Preferred Stock: 0.1%
  • Options: 0.1%

Expanding on this diversity, the fund also maintains an efficient allocation across different sectors. For instance, CSQ is most heavily allocated to information technology companies at 27.1% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to financials and consumer discretionary investments, which accounts for 13.2% and 11.2% of assets respectively. This diversity helps mitigate any sort of concentration risks to any one specific focus area.

Another unique aspect of the fund is the direct equity exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, the largest holding is currently Nvidia (NVDA) at 5.2% of assets. This is followed by other notable names, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Broadcom (AVGO), just to name a few. CSQ's choice to include so many growth stocks means that investors can essentially collect a high yield from a pool of securities, which isn't normally possible with more traditional ETFs.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since CSQ is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, CSQ currently trades at a discount to NAV of 7.78%. For reference, this is slightly larger than the discount to NAV of 4.7% at the time of my last coverage. Additionally, CSQ has traded at an average discount to NAV of 1.1% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that CSQ current trades at one of the most attractive price to NAV levels in relation to its ten-year range. I believe that this price disconnect can indicate that it remains a good time to accumulate shares.

Since my last coverage, CSQ has released an updated annual report for 2025. This is the most updated report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to a loss of $0.18 per share, while net realized gains totaled $4.69 per share. Looking back on prior years, the fund has consistently experienced poor net investment income and I believe this can be attributed to the higher interest rate environment. Coincidentally, net investment income was positive when interest rates were at near zero levels back in 2021. Since rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023, net investment income started to amount to losses. This can be an indication of the increased operating costs.

My main issue is that the poor level of net investment income means that the fund is entirely reliant on positive net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distributions. This means that the fund has no reliable protection against market declines and may find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually earns in income when markets are declining. This also makes it difficult to estimate the rate of NAV growth that may occur through different market cycles. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $18.62 per share
  • 2022: $13.57 per share
  • 2023: $13.41 per share
  • 2024: $17.55 per share
  • 2025: $20.83 per share

The only challenge is that the current structure means that CSQ isn't capable of allowing its best positions to grow uninterrupted. Since the fund is reliant on net realized gains, CSQ has to actively sell off portions of the upside growth from its positions. Over a longer holding period, this translates to a limited level of price growth. For instance, CSQ's share price has increased by 125.7% over the last decade. While this level of price growth is solid, we can see that the total return when including all distributions sits at 408.7% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that ~75% of their total return will come from distributions, while only the remaining ~25% will come from actual price growth.

Another challenge of the current structure is that the fund has no way of generating income during market declines. Since net investment income is so low, it is not a reliable source of income during the current market cycle of high interest rates. Therefore, CSQ may find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually earns in income, which leads to large losses during declines. A good example of this was demonstrated through 2022's market decline. As we can see below, CSQ saw a total loss of 24.25% over 2022, despite having a high dividend yield. The fund underperformed a more traditional ETF like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) over the same time frame.

Lastly, the fund's use of leverage can also amplify losses if allocated to securities that do not perform as anticipated. The use of leverage currently amounts to 29.45% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$1.37B. I consider this to be an aggressive amount of leverage in this higher interest rate environment, which was already proven by the fund's poor net investment income over the last few years.

This is simply because the operating spread between CSQ's debt and the yield collected from securities becomes much thinner. Although the Fed has initiated three different 25 basis point cuts in 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. While future interest rates can serve as a positive growth catalyst, we don't know how much longer it will take for rates to get reduced back down to their pre-pandemic levels.

Dividend Strength

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1225 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.47 per share. Referring to the same annual report, we can see that CSQ has no issues generating earnings that can outpace the distributions being paid. For reference, here are the annual earnings over the last few years:

  • 2021: $6.52 per share
  • 2022: ($3.82) per share
  • 2023: $1.07 per share
  • 2024: $5.37 per share
  • 2025: $4.51 per share

As we can see, the fund only struggled to generate an efficient level of earnings in 2022 and 2023. However, this is understandable given the uncertainty around the pandemic, interest rates, and inflationary data. I really like the fact that the fund is capable of generating earnings that can generate several years worth of distributions in a single period. This was most recently demonstrated throughout the 2025 period:

2025's earnings of $4.51 per share / $1.47 annual payout = 3.06 years.

More impressively, CSQ has been able to provide slight dividend growth over time. This is usual for a closed-end fund that offers a high starting dividend yield, so I think this makes the fund extremely desirable for investors that want to prioritize income stability and reliability. Even through the uncertainty of the pandemic, the fund has been able to keep distributions steady.

This consistency also allows investors to grow their dividend income over time. To demonstrate this, I ran a back test of an original $100,000 investment at the start of 2016. This calculation assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $100K. However, the calculation does assume that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of more shares. So by simply remaining invested and reinvesting dividends, long-term investors would have seen their annual dividend income more than double.

2016 Dividend Income: $10,485

2025 Dividend Income: $27,266

Furthermore, we can see that the price and NAV both increased over this same time range. The price increased by nearly 94% , while the underlying NAV increased by 84.6% over the same time frame. When including all of the distributions that were paid out, we can see that total return was nearly 337%. During this entire period, the fund maintained positive returns, despite volatility through the pandemic era. This is all a testament to the fund's strength and the efficiency of its strategy over a longer holding period.

Lastly, CSQ is great for income investors because the fund prioritizes tax-efficient distributions. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 100% of the distributions paid YTD were classified as long-term capital gains, which are typically considered qualified dividends and have the most favorable tax rate for investors. This provides flexibility for investors that want to prioritize income while limiting their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on CSQ at this time. The fund actually trades at a larger discount to NAV valuation compared to the time of my last coverage, despite market indices rising to new all-time highs. The larger discount to NAV pricing can be an indication that long-term investors can accumulate shares to take advantage of this disconnect. The fund continues to deliver NAV growth and generate earnings that far exceed the distributions being paid. For these reasons, CSQ is still a great buy-and hold fund for income investors seeking income stability and reliability over time.

data.title: 'Garbage Loans'; What A Prudent Investor Should Know
data.description:
Following the unexpected defaults of Tricolor Holdings and First Brands late last summer, Jamie Dimon famously quipped that there would be more 'cockroaches' that would emerge from the private credit market. It is easy to see why Mr. Dimon could have been irked. JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), UBS and Jefferies Financial Group Inc. (JEF) were among the banks that took significant write-offs in connection to these debacles.

In addition, the private credit market has exploded over the past decade and a half. In response to the Great Financial Crisis, new regulations were put in place that made it more problematic for banks to hold riskier loans. This has increasingly sidelined regional and major banks from a lucrative niche of the credit markets.

While the CEO of JPMorgan got the headlines for his colorful statement around private credit, I prefer what noted bond manager and billionaire Jeffrey Gundlach said a month later about this sector. He noted that private lenders were making a lot of "garbage loans". He also predicted that the next financial crisis will emanate from the private credit space.

Private credit is basically lending done by nonbank institutions and is also known as 'shadow banking.' According to an article on CNBC this week, the private credit market hit $3.4 trillion in 2025 and will reach nearly $5 trillion by 2029. Morgan Stanley currently pegs the size of private credit at $3 trillion. Obviously, given its size, the market could have substantial impacts on the markets and the U.S. economy if things went south.

And unfortunately, this space is much less transparent or far more opaque than banks like Citigroup (C), Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) and JP Morgan Chase. These entities have to file quarterly reports with the S.E.C. and are subject to numerous other regulatory and compliance requirements. This makes banks much easier for investors to monitor for potential problems emerging.

And this is why problems in private credit can blindside investors and the markets. There were some, but not many, clues that First Brands was in trouble before it filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Fitch downgraded the debt just a few days before its bankruptcy filing. This aftermarket auto parts supplier had some $9 billion in debt, of which $5 billion were in first-lien loans, $500 million in second-lien loans, $2 billion in off-balance sheet obligations, and $2 billion in direct factoring arrangements. One of the company's lenders made an emergency court filing. This called for an independent investigation into the over $2 billion the lender stated had "simply vanished." A 'cockroach' indeed.

And while banks may not fund these entities directly, they do provide funding to warehouse facilities and other lenders that do. Which is JP Morgan Chase and other banks had write-offs in connection with these two high-profile bankruptcies.

Trillions of dollars were lent out to businesses and real estate developers and investors during the ZIRP period following Covid that existed to when the Federal Reserve started to lift rates in March of 2022. It would be naive to think that more garbage loans won't be exposed as this debt has to be refinanced at much higher rates in the quarters and years ahead.

We can see what this stress might look like by viewing what has happened in commercial real estate, or CRE debt markets, where there is some $4.8 trillion of debt outstanding in the United States. The delinquency rate on Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities, or CMBS has spiked to roughly 12%. This is higher than the peak experienced during the Great Financial Crisis.

The CMBS delinquency rate on multifamily properties has just over doubled to near seven percent over the past five quarters. Together these two CRE sectors account for roughly $3.5 trillion of the nearly $5 trillion of CRE debt outstanding.

And the scary part of these delinquency rates is they do not include the various loan modifications lenders are providing on these debt obligations. How extensive could this type of 'extend & pretend' activity be right now? According to Morningstar's commercial real estate outlook for 2026 that just came out, there is just over $100 billion of CMBS whose debt will mature this year. Of which, Morningstar estimates that just over half ($57.6 billion) will have a maturity default. Nearly $10 billion more of these debt maturities are on the 'cusp between default and payoff.'

Even more disconcerting is that Morningstar ran an analysis on a roughly 1,250 loan dataset to project the average value decline from the original appraisal on the underlying property to the most recent appraisal on that same asset. The results are above.

These datapoints don't seem to come up much on CNBC or on the rest of the financial media. However, given the importance of the health of the credit markets to equities and the U.S. economy, prudent investors should be monitoring these markets closely. I used external research sites to get free updates on the commercial real estate market on a regular basis.

A prudent investor can also read through the quarterly transcripts of Business Development Companies, or BDCs, which provide loans to a diverse set of businesses and industries, as a good way to keep an eye on the health of the non-bank-funded credit markets. Two of the largest BDCs are Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). Tellingly, both of these entities are down some 20% over the past 12 months. And this happened as Chairman Powell has taken the Fed Funds rate down by 175bps cumulatively now since he started to reduce rates in September of 2024.

I also continue to look for podcasters that post frequently, provide great datapoints, and have a good handle on the private credit market on YouTube. I think it is important as a prudent investor to monitor the credit markets closely, as trouble here invariably finds its way to impact the equity markets eventually.

data.title: Hoegh LNG Partners: I'm Firmly Holding The High-Yield Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

I have owned a long position in the preferred shares of Hoegh LNG Partners (HMLPF) for several years now. A portion of that period was frustrating, not in the least because there was uncertainty as to how the owners of the company (technically, the partnership) would want to deal with the preferred shareholders. A recent decision to buy back the preferred shares, in combination with HMLPF not skipping a single preferred dividend payment, makes me more optimistic.

A robust result means excellent dividend coverage

Before discussing the preferred shares and the (results of the) tender offer for the preferred shares, I obviously wanted to have a look at the financial performance of the partnership, as that, of course, ultimately decides on the dividend coverage levels and the safety of the balance sheet.

In the third quarter of 2025 – we obviously still have to wait for HMLPF to release its Q4 results—the partnership reported total revenue of just under $36M, a decrease of approximately $3M compared to the third quarter of 2024. This was partially compensated for by lower operating expenses and higher earnings from joint ventures, and this kept the operating income decrease limited to just $1M, or less than 4%.

Meanwhile, the net interest expenses continue to decrease: Whereas Hoegh LNG Partners reported a net interest expense of $3M in Q3 2024, this decreased to just $2M in Q3 2025, which means the pre-tax income came in flat, and the net income increased by a little bit due to a lower tax bill.

The net profit in Q3 was approximately $23M, of which $3.9M was required to cover the preferred dividends. This resulted in a payout ratio of approximately 17% of the earnings.

Looking at the balance sheet, we see the partnership had approximately $35M in cash (but a working capital deficit of approximately $7M), while the long-term debt decreased as well. The total amount of equity on the balance sheet came in at $662M, of which $177M (I use the $25 call price to determine the preferred equity) was preferred equity and $485M was common equity.

The total amount of equity on the balance sheet decreased by approximately $11M, and that’s because the partners received a $62M distribution in the first nine months of the year. This happened before the summer, as the Q3 distribution on the common equity was a very reasonable $12M.

So, from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, I'm quite happy with both the preferred dividend coverage levels and the balance sheet situation, as there’s almost $500M in common equity, which ranks junior to the preferred equity.

The recent preferred stock buyback actually makes the preferred shares safer

Hoegh LNG Partners has one series of preferred shares outstanding, and this security is trading with HMLPF as its ticker symbol. The preferred shares offer an 8.75% preferred dividend yield based on the $25 principal value, and the quarterly dividends of almost $0.55 result in a current yield of 11.2% based on the current share price of $19.52. After the recent tender offer for preferred stock, there are just under 5.7M preferred shares outstanding.

It was also interesting to see the partnership launching a tender offer to buy back up to $35M of the preferred stock, at a fixed price of $19.50 per unit. A total of 1.4M preferred shares were tendered into this offer, and all shares were taken up by Hoegh LNG for a total cash outflow of $27.2M.

The tender offer was not fully subscribed, as most preferred shareholders, including myself, are waiting to see what the next steps will be for Hoegh. As long as the preferred dividends continue to flow, I’m not particularly interested in tendering my preferred stock. After all, Hoegh LNG Partners can call the entire series at $25 per share if it really wants to remove the securities from the capital stack.

Retiring the preferred shares through a tender offer has also made the security safer for the remaining preferred shareholders: The dividend coverage ratio should improve as HMLPF will save about $2.4M per year in preferred dividends.

And using the 9M 2025 results, we will see an increase in the preferred equity versus total equity ratio. That ratio stood at 26.7% at the end of Q3, and will now decrease to $142M vs. $635M for a ratio of just over 22%. By retiring preferred stock at a discount to the call price, makes a preferred investment safer for the remaining preferred equity holders.

Investment thesis

I have a long position in HMLPF, and I'm continuing to hold my entire position. I would only consider participating in a tender offer if the offered price is closer to $25. I don’t expect that to happen, as the Hoegh LNG Partners are incentivized to try to retire the preferred stock as cheap as possible.

As I recently took quite a bit of profit in other sectors (mainly mining), I'm looking to continue to increase my position in fixed income. HMLPF ranks high on my shortlist, and although I have a full position, I think any weakness is an opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SGOV Is Still A Tool For Cash But The Yield Is Declining
data.description:
When investors look for places to park cash, they often look toward assets that offer as close to a risk-free rate of return as possible. These are assets that offer minimal credit risk, have high liquidity, and have minimal price volatility. They also look to generate income from leaving cash in these vehicles. The iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) is designed to be a vehicle that incorporates these features through a portfolio of short-dated U.S. Treasury securities. SGOV is still interesting for investors looking to squeeze out additional income from their cash on hand, but this is not a replacement for capital appreciation or building an income-producing portfolio. SGOV is an effective way to own a rolling portfolio of T-bills without having to do the work yourself through an ETF. There is over $70 billion sitting in SGOV but as the Fed continues to cut rates, the yield continues to decline. SGOV may still look attractive with a 4.09% yield, but the annualized yield is likely to fall below 4% in 2026 which could reduce the income opportunity that many investors have become accustomed to. I think that as 2026 unfolds, SGOV will lose some of its appeal, and it will no longer be used as a way to farm income rather than allocating capital to equities, and its primary use case will be a proxy for cash once again.

Following up on my previous article about SGOV

In April of 2025 I had written an article on SGOV (can be read here) where I discussed how the yield was still attractive but the upcoming rate cuts would impact its viability. I believed that while SGOV was a reliable source of generating income, investors would be able to generate more yield and potential upside through the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) as the Fed cut rates. SGOV's dividend income has dropped from over 5% to 4.37%, and longer duration bonds were becoming attractive again. I am following up with a new article to discuss how the investment case for SGOV is drying up, and as the forward yield falls under 4%, it will likely be looked at as a proxy for cash rather than a viable option to generate income.

What SGOV is, how it works, and why it's been beneficial for investors seeking income

SGOV is an ETF that holds U.S. Treasuries with a duration of less than 3 months. It is designed to act as a rolling portfolio of bonds that continuously updates and does the work for the shareholder. There is an expense ratio of 0.09% and a dividend that is paid monthly. Currently, SGOV is paying $4.12 per share on a trailing twelve-month (TTM) basis, which is a 4.09% dividend yield. This is attractive to many investors because interest rates had gotten so low that earning a meaningful amount of capital from short-duration proxies for cash was nonexistent for a while.

SGOV holds Treasuries that mature in less than 3 months, and the portfolio is rebalanced each month. SGOV uses an indexing approach where the portfolio is constantly rolling the proceeds from maturing bonds into newly issued short-duration bonds or ones that have 3 months or less until maturity. SGOV is a short duration ETF and there is often a step pattern that is established in the share price. This is where the share price gradually rises as interest accrues and then falls when the ex-dividend date occurs as accrued income is paid out.

SGOV has become a tool for income investors because they are gaining credit exposure with minimal duration risk. Treasuries are generally treated as the baseline for credit, but longer-duration bonds can be more volatile when rates move. SGOV stays at the front end of duration, which allows its volatility to remain low. SGOV is often looked at as a cash management tool inside of brokerage accounts because it's easy to move in and out of it with a share price that has been predictable. Buying SGOV is an effective way to manage cash and generate income rather than buying bonds directly and having to stay on top of the maturity dates. For a low expense ratio, the fund does all the work for a small fee that is paid prior to the dividends. SGOV has won over investors when considering other risk-free assets for several reasons. Compared to T-bills, it allows no ladder maintenance, intraday liquidity, and the ability to add or reduce the investment quickly.

Why I believe SGOV will be looked at as a proxy for cash rather than an income producer

Due to the Fed’s hiking cycle, SGOV’s dividend grew at a quick pace, but those days are gone. In 2021, SGOV generated $0.03 in dividend income, which increased 4,532% in 2022 to $1.45. Then in 2023, SGOV’s annualized payout increased 236% to $4.88. In 2024 investors truly embraced the T-bill and chill methodology as SGOV grew the dividend by 5% to $5.11 and the effective yield exceeded 5%. Now that 2025 is over, we watched the dividend fall -20% to $4.12 and SGOV’s distribution yield fall. The last 3 monthly dividends averaged $0.33 and based on this data, the forward dividend if rates don’t move would be $3.93 for 2026. SGOV is likely to produce less than 4% on the dividend in 2026 for the first time in 3 years, and that’s only the beginning.

The most recent Fed Dot Plot shows rates falling to around 3.25% in 2026 and reaching 3% in 2027. I think that this will dramatically change once a new Fed Chair is appointed. The Truflation index is down to 1.2%, and we have seen Core CPI fall to 2.6%, which is a lagging indicator. Oil has been hovering between $55-$65 per barrel, and rampant inflation has been eliminated. The new Fed Chair and President Trump will likely be aligned in a lower rate policy, and this could give the current members of the Fed who are doves more power and control over the narrative. I believe that we will get 3 cuts in 2026 and 3 cuts in 2027 as we work our way down to the 2.25% level. As this occurs, SGOV will lose more of its remaining appeal and only be useful as a proxy for cash, as it won’t be able to compete with many income producing investments in the equities market.

There are too many investments in the market that are generating yields that exceed the risk-free rate of return to remain in a deteriorating vehicle such as SGOV. Verizon (VZ), for instance, is paying $2.76 per share on the dividend, which is a 6.98% yield. People are scared of VZ because of the $127 billion in long-term debt on the balance sheet; the reality is that they are exceeding $50 billion in annualized EBITDA which puts their leverage ratio at 2.53x. VZ also produces $21.12 billion in FCF and after paying $11.41 billion in dividends, there is still $9.71 billion in retained FCF to pay down debt. As rates decline, VZ could become more popular as an income investment, especially as SGOV starts to have a forward yield that progressively declines towards 3%.

Risks to investing in SGOV

I don’t believe there are many structural risks to investing in SGOV from a perspective of losing money. If short-duration U.S. Treasuries decline in value and SGOV falls under $100 per share, then we probably have much larger problems to deal with. I think the real risk to investing in SGOV is not understanding how it works and how it's affected by the yield curve. We’re not in an environment where the Fed is taking rates higher, and SGOV is unlikely to generate the same amount of income as it did last year. Investors should be careful adding anything other than cash that they want to keep on hand to SGOV as this is likely to return less income in 2026 than in 2025 and less in 2027 than in 2026.

Conclusion

I think that SGOV is a great vehicle to allocate on-hand liquidity to with the purpose of squeezing out a mid-single-digit yield without the time restrictions. The nice thing about SGOV rather than a direct bond or CD is that you can sell it intraday at any time and extract your capital without penalties. Outside of making your cash work for you that you want to keep on hand, I see limited reasons to have SGOV as part of an income producing portfolio as the amount of income being produced continues to fall. Investors should look at alternatives as the Fed takes rates lower because SGOV is trending in a downward trajectory, and this is unlikely to change anytime soon.

data.title: American Greed: Show Me The Honest Dividends
data.description:
Co-authored with Dislocation Capital

You work hard to earn money. You finally deploy it into a company with eye-popping earnings growth, glowing forecasts, and a story that feels like it could genuinely change your financial trajectory. And then, out of the blue, an accounting/reporting issue surfaces, regulators get involved, confidence evaporates, and the stock price drops. You then begin to see news articles about a dozen law firms pursuing lawsuits against management regarding potential breaches of fiduciary duties, inviting shareholders with losses to participate. This represents a potential scenario where years of discipline can be undone in days.

A recent example here at High Dividend Opportunities illustrates this dynamic all too clearly. Compass Diversified (CODI), a diversified holding company with audited financials and multiple operating subsidiaries, disclosed that one of its businesses (Lugano) required a material restatement of its financials. Sales and profits were overstated, inventory was misrepresented, liabilities were kept off the books, and revenues were inflated. Once the numbers were restated, reported revenues at the subsidiary collapsed by more than 80%, revealing a business far smaller than investors believed they owned. Allegations are flying, and Bloomberg reports that the FBI is probing the deals that were central to the inaccurate financials.

This isn’t some rare, bad-luck outcome. Whether due to intentional fraud, incompetence, honest mistakes, or fudging, SEC filings aren't always accurate. Evidence below suggests that it can be a more structural issue, with ~11% of large U.S. public companies engaged in corporate fraud at any point. To avoid getting impacted, we believe that investors can benefit from thinking more like Rod Tidwell (played by Cuba Gooding Jr.) in Jerry Maguire—less impressed by lofty promises and more focused on a simple demand: “Show me the money.” In markets, this can mean real cash returned to shareholders, not just reported earnings or long-dated forecasts. In this article, we explore research on the prevalence of fraud and how income investing can help reduce our exposure to fraud.

Why Should We Care?

Corporate fraud is far more pervasive than what investors can usually see. Research exploring the collapse of Arthur Anderson after Enron, an event that subjected their former clients to intense scrutiny, estimated that around 11% of large U.S. public companies are engaged in corporate fraud at any point in time, with only a few (about one-third) of these ever detected. The annual costs of these types of fraud were estimated at 1.7% of total U.S. equity market value, representing billions of dollars of shareholder value quietly eroded per year.

Looking deeper into the types of fraud, more recent evidence from the Association of Certified Fraud Examiners shows that though financial statement fraud is less prevalent than the other types of occupational fraud (asset misappropriation and corruption), it can be the most expensive (losses from financial statement fraud are calculated using the gross amount of the financial misstatement reported, whereas losses for all other fraud schemes reflect estimated direct financial losses incurred) to the company, as these schemes often persist over multiple reporting periods (median of 18 months) and affect a broad set of stakeholders, resulting in a median loss of approximately $766k per case compared to $200k or less for other cases, with reported loss severity increasing sharply since 2022.

Financial statement fraud rarely occurs in isolation and is frequently accompanied by other forms of occupational fraud.

Fraud detection is driven primarily by internal tips and employee reporting, while external investors are structurally disadvantaged in uncovering misconduct until it has already been internally exposed or publicly disclosed.

Overall, fraud is prevalent, loss severity has risen since 2022, multiple types of fraud often occur together, and detection depends more on internal mechanisms, with very limited opportunity for external shareholders like us to spot.

Specifically focusing on financial statement fraud, the underlying driver appears to be misalignment between managers and shareholders. When managers control capital but own relatively less of the business or are provided incentives to chase short-term performance, they can tilt towards appearances—earnings management, aggressive accounting, & selective disclosure.

Our intuition has always been that dividends require hard cash, and since fabricated earnings can’t easily be used for these purposes, there must be some truth to the assertion that fraud is less likely among companies that pay dividends. Now, let's explore if there is any evidence that forcing the companies to regularly “show the money” through dividends can lead to less storytelling, more cash, and possibly fewer unpleasant surprises.

Why “Show Me The Money” Matters In Practice

Our first question revolved around whether firms accused of accounting fraud were less likely to pay dividends.

Research investigating this very question, using companies cited by the SEC for fraudulent investing, found that dividend-paying firms are significantly less likely to be accused of fraud in the first place. In the year before the alleged fraud period, only 17% of fraud firms paid a dividend versus 23% of comparable non-fraud firms. Though the difference looks small, statistical analysis revealed that simply being a dividend payer reduced the unconditional probability of being accused of fraud by 30%. The comparison accounted for differences in size, industry, and other observable characteristics of these firms. Even after controlling for these factors, the dividend signal remained strong. Beyond just the presence of dividends, these fraudulent firms paid dividends less often, increased them less frequently, and cut them more often than matched dividend payers. For investors, a consistently increasing dividend sends a clearer signal of trustworthy financials.

The second question: Do dividend-paying firms face less pressure to play the earnings game?

Research on this topic has shown that markets respond differently to earnings announcements for dividend-payers versus non-payers. Dividend-paying firms experienced 11% to 15% lower earnings response coefficients, meaning investors revise expectations less aggressively after earnings announcements. This isn’t because investors are complacent—it's because dividends already communicate information about permanent and sustainable earnings, reducing the incremental value of each quarterly earnings print. With less market information focused on beating numbers, managers have less incentive to manipulate earnings, and conference calls also become less centered on hitting these earnings thresholds. These patterns persist even when considering potential confounding explanations like firm maturity, size, or cash holdings.

Taken together, these findings reinforce the core message of this article that forcing companies to regularly “show the money” through dividends can lead to less storytelling, more cash, and possibly fewer unpleasant surprises. It’s important to note that this does not indicate that dividend-paying firms are magically immune to fraud—but they operate in an environment where fraud is harder to sustain, earnings manipulation is less rewarded, and reported numbers could be more trustworthy on average. Rod Tidwell didn’t want projections. He wanted proof. Dividends don’t eliminate risk—but they force companies to prove, quarter after quarter, that the money is real.

The HDO Approach

A key takeaway for investors is that fraud is going to happen and is tough to predict, but our style of investing helps limit the impact. Here at HDO, we have seen and have been impacted by fraud in several scenarios and have learned to defend ourselves in multiple ways beyond just focusing on income-paying securities.

We focus on spreading capital across multiple income-producing businesses, which reduces the damage when individual companies commit fraud, continuing to showcase our belief that diversification is the only free lunch in investing.

We focus on investing in the higher levels of the issuer’s capital structure, which has also proven to be important for protection—showcased in both our recent experience with CODI preferreds, where the company continues to exist and pay us while the situation is investigated. Or our historical experiences with the entanglement of BRC Holdings Group (RILY) with the fraud committed by the then CEO of The Franchise Group, Brian Kahn, who pleaded guilty last month. We invested in the bonds, and while it has been a wild ride, the company is moving on, and bond prices have materially recovered from the initial panic.

Sometimes the market's overreaction to fraud-related news creates a buying opportunity, like when we bought VEREIT (previously ARCP), where the CFO was found guilty of falsifying AFFO. While the conviction was a huge blow to the stock, our realization that the real physical assets would provide a margin of safety allowed us to buy both the common and preferred after the fraud was revealed. Finally, we realized a nice gain when it was acquired by Realty Income (O).

At HDO, we don’t make speculative bets. Instead, we use simple powers to fight fraud, as our investments are anchored on fundamentals, and we demand our companies to “show us the money” every quarter. You can't always avoid these situations, but you can position your portfolio to limit the impact these situations have on your portfolio. And sometimes they are even profitable buying opportunities.

data.title: Analyst Connect January 2026: Outlining Expectations For ETF Coverage
data.description:
Reminders and Updates on ETF Coverage

One of the things Seeking Alpha has been working on over the past year is increasing both the quality and quantity of our ETF coverage. It’s our desire to make Seeking Alpha a go-to destination for ETF content. With this in mind, we’re trying to make sure our analysis stands out. Having said that, we want to reiterate our editorial standards for ETF analysis as well as offer additional guidance. As a reminder, our standard of delivering original, actionable insights pertains to ETF analysis articles just as it does to articles on stocks.

We encourage analysts who write about ETFs to bring their forward-looking guidance into the spotlight more consistently and clearly. We’re looking for analysts to illustrate how an ETF’s construction and methodology are applied to their views. In doing so, it’s important to explain that ETF holdings are typically dynamic, and as such, are impacted by rebalancing frequency, selection criteria, caps, and other elements. Integrating the discussions around the holdings and the methodology that shapes them communicates your robust understanding of both and adds credibility to your recommendation. And, similarly to stock article requirements, adding quantitative detail to your foreseeable outcome would make your recommendation more tangible and therefore more clearly tradable — this would include a discussion of NAV, total return (and how much of that would be attributed to price appreciation and income), average/weighted average P/E when applicable, the expense ratio, and other elements that impact tradability, like AUM.

Also, keep in mind that an ETF pair trade or general comparative discussions on ETFs should also contain a decisive recommendation based on a definitive outlook, not only an illustration of how hypothetical market dynamics affect each ETF differently.

Our guidelines also apply to coverage of newer “enhanced income” ETFs that have spurred both analysts’ and readers’ interest. Some of these options-overlay ETFs are complex in their methodology and require additional background information to illustrate how they operate. However, we want to stress that articles focusing primarily on this aspect fall short of reader expectations regarding forward-looking, actionable, and original insights. We invite analysts who choose to cover these enhanced-income ETFs to let their forward-looking guidance take center stage and frame their analysis around their main thesis, just as they would other ETF or stock articles.

Analysts should break down that complexity behind these ETFs into digestible information for readers, drilling into the “why” behind the anticipated outcomes in the thesis. Since these ETFs have some unique features, analysts should include some additional points above typical ETFs they’re used to covering and should:

  • Clearly explain the options strategy in sufficient detail

  • Explain the relationship to the underlying and the holdings

  • Describe the distributions and their effects, including their tax character and frequency

  • Address the likelihood and drivers of NAV erosion/drag

  • Avoid thin "apples to oranges" comparisons; instead, illustrate the effects of strategy differences

  • Note key catalysts (volatility events, valuation extremes) and expected behavior across market regimes.

  • Describe the ETF’s role in a portfolio

It’s important that these elements are woven together in a cohesive story and not added in as disjointed points.

To drive consistency across articles in adherence to this standard, we’re extending additional editorial time to guide analysts who would need to elevate their ETF analysis to better meet these guidelines. This would in turn drive views and follower counts, as readers would appreciate a consistent, actionable takeaway from ETF coverage.

Additional resources are available in previous editions of the Analyst Connect newsletter. Check out Jack Bowman’s ideas for ETF coverage, along with updates on our minimums for ETF coverage.

Putting a Bigger Focus on Sectors: Expanding the Use of ETFs as Proxies

We’ve achieved a lot of success adding key index ETFs as primaries for market outlook articles. We’re expanding the use of ETFs to bring attention to different sectors.

We’ve developed a list of sector/industry ETFs that we will use as primary tickers in articles focused specifically on that sector. We’ll add these tickers for articles that present a broader, high-level “macro” look at a sector. The sectors we’ve identified (along with the ETFs):

US Financials

XLF, VFH, IYF, FNCL

US Banks

KBE, KBWB, XLF

US Regional Banks

KBWR, KRE, IAT, DPST

US Communications

XLC, IYZ, VOX, FCOM

US Consumer Discretionary

XLY, VCR, IYC, FDIS

US Consumer Staples

XLP, VDC, FSTA, KXI

US Energy

XLE, VDE, IYE, FENY

US Healthcare

XLV, VHT, IYH, FHLC

US Utilities

XLU, VPU, FUTY, IDU

US Industrials

XLI, IYJ, VIS, FIDU

US Materials

XLB, VAW, IYM, FMAT

US Real Estate

VNQ, IYR, SCHH, XLRE

US Software & Services

IGV, XSW, HACK

US Technology

XLK, VGT, IYW, IGM

Defense & Aerospace

ITA, XAR, PPA, SHLD

Biotech

IBB, XBI, BBH

Semiconductors

SOXX, SMH, XSD, SOXQ

Gold

GLD, IAU, GLDM, SGOL

Silver

SLV, PSLV, SIVR

Oil

USO, XOP, BNO, IEO

We’re limiting the use of these tickers to 3-4 well-followed ETFs for each sector. We’ll tag these articles “Market Outlook.” Also, note that these tickers are not eligible for undercovered bonuses when used for sector coverage.

We encourage analysts to consider sector analysis as a way to expand their macro coverage. Email submissions@seekingalpha.com with any questions or ideas.

Looking for Short Ideas

We encourage analysts to submit their short ideas.

Please see our reminders and suggestions for short ideas:

We do not allow analysts to repeat allegations made by others (such as short sellers). While you are free to briefly mention the existence of a short report (ideally with a link to a Seeking Alpha news story about it so readers can see that for background), you should focus on your thesis.

We suggest reviewing the editorial policies for short ideas here.

If you have any questions about our short ideas policies in general, please email us at submissions@seekingalpha.com. Also, if you’re considering submitting a short idea and you’re not sure if it would be a good fit for any reason (such as a low market cap or a key claim you are making), to help avoid any wasted work on your part, you can email us prior to submitting the article, and we will provide preliminary feedback.

We’re looking forward to seeing your ideas.

New Article Competition: Best Long Idea for 2026

Seeking Alpha has a new article competition in place for the New Year: Top 2026 Long Idea Competition.

Details on this competition can be found here.

The deadline is Feb. 28. Make sure to add a note for editors regarding the entry. We’re looking for a clear and compelling thesis that highlights why this is your top long idea for 2026. The article must be rated a buy or strong buy. And the article must be focused on an individual stock, not an ETF or cryptocurrency.

More on our article competitions can be found here.

How to Build a Following at Seeking Alpha? Persistence!

Seeking Alpha analyst and Investing Group leader Victor Dergunov has provided his commentary and analysis since 2017. In that time, Dergunov has amassed more than 54,000 followers and posted more than 1,000 articles. The commitment to the site opened the door for Dergunov to launch The Financial Prophet, a service that allows him to share investment ideas and portfolio strategy with others.

Dergunov says his efforts have always focused on helping readers and members of his investing group, delivering original, actionable investment ideas. To achieve success on Seeking Alpha, Dergunov suggests analysts be patient with the process and build relationships with editors and the community.

Most of all, Dergunov says analysts should form a persistent attitude, committing to a long-term perspective that generates interest in the articles presented to readers.

Dergunov shares his pointers in this article.

Looking Ahead to February

Earnings season is in full swing as we head into February with a number of highly followed and high market-cap names set to report throughout the month. The news cycle will continue to spin as U.S. policies are creating a great deal of volatility in recent weeks, and that's likely to continue this month as well. As always, as we get further out in the month, some of the earnings dates haven’t been confirmed by the companies yet, and these are estimated dates.

Seeking Alpha Earnings Calendar

Week 1 (Feb. 2-6)

February starts with a bang, as on the first trading day of the month there are 15 companies with a market cap greater than $10B slated to report. Palantir (PLTR) is the headliner, but Disney (DIS), Simon Property Group (SPG), NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX), and ON Semiconductor (ON) are also on the docket for Feb. 2. Tuesday is even busier, as 45 companies worth more than $10B are expected to report, and eight $100B companies lead the way. The notable names at the top in terms of market cap are Amazon (AMZN), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Merck (MRK), PepsiCo (PEP), Amgen (AMGN), Pfizer (PFE), Eaton (ETN), and Chubb (CB).

The reports don’t slow down on Wednesday either; in fact, it gets even busier. There are 12 companies with +$100B market caps reporting and 61 with market caps north of $10B. The most anticipated one is likely Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), but we also get healthcare heavyweights Eli Lilly (LLY), AbbVie (ABBV), and Novartis (NVS). Other notables reporting include Uber (UBER), Qualcomm (QCOM), and Arm Holdings (ARM). Thursday is just as busy, with seven $100B companies expected to report and 61 with market caps of more than $10B. Shell (SHEL), Sony (SONY), ConocoPhillips, and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) highlight Thursday’s reports. Friday does show a slowdown, but we still have reports expected from the likes of Toyota (TM), Philip Morris International (PM), and Kenvue (KVUE).

Week 2 (Feb. 9-14)

Earnings reports slow a little in the second week, with Monday showing 13 companies with market caps over $10B. Apollo Global Management (APO) leads the way in terms of market cap and other highly followed names include Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF), and monday.com (MNDY). The action picks back up on Tuesday with seven $100B companies set to report. They are led by Coca-Cola (KO), AstraZeneca (AZN), S&P Global (SPGI), and Gilead Sciences (GILD). Other notable companies reporting include Robinhood (HOOD), BP (BP), Ferrari (RACE) and Ford (F).

Wednesday doesn’t have as many high market cap names reporting, but the ones at the top are very highly followed: Cisco Systems (CSCO), McDonald’s (MCD), T-Mobile (TMUS), AppLovin (APP), and Shopify (SHOP) all have significant followers. Looking out to Thursday, we have reports from Applied Materials (AMAT), Arista Networks (ANET), Southern Copper (SCCO), Unilever (UL), and Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) on the docket. There are a few companies showing on the calendar for Friday, but we weren’t able to confirm any of the highly followed/high market cap names.

Week 3 (Feb. 16-20)

U.S. markets are closed on Monday, Feb. 16, in observance of President’s Day. Looking at Tuesday’s docket, there are a number of $10B companies reporting, but the only $100B company set to report is Medtronic (MDT). Other notable names include Energy Transfer (ET), Keysight Technologies (KEYS), Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), and Devon Energy (DVN). Wednesday is slightly busier, with highly followed names like Booking Holdings (BKNG), Analog Devices (ADI), Carvana (CVNA), DoorDash (DASH), and Baidu (BIDU) expected to report.

Thursday marks a transition to retail and consumer discretionary names as Walmart (WMT) will kick things off in the sector. We also have notables like Deere & Company (DE), Newmont Mining (NEM), and AngloGold Ashanti (AU). Friday shows Alibaba (BABA) and Constellation Energy (CEG) set to report, but the investor relations pages for both companies have not confirmed the date.

Week 4 (Feb. 23-27)

The final week of February sees earnings reports starting slowly but building as the week goes on. Monday’s docket shows American Tower (AMT), Dominion Energy (D), and Trip.com (TCOM) all expected to report. Tuesday sees the activity in the consumer discretionary sector resume with Home Depot (HD) and MercadoLibre (MELI) leading the charge. We also expect to hear from Autodesk (ADSK) and Zoom (ZM).

Wednesday is a big day, as the last of the Mag 7 names will step forward, as Nvidia (NVDA) is expected to report at that time. We also expect to hear from retailers TJX (TJX) and Lowe’s (LOW) that day, along with tech companies Salesforce (CRM) and Snowflake (SNOW). We see more tech names expected on Thursday with Intuit (INTU), Dell Technologies (DELL), and Workday (WDAY) all slated to report. Other notables for the day are Monster Beverage (MNST) and NetEase (NTES). The current calendar shows Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) reporting on Friday, but those announcements usually take place on Saturdays.

data.title: Liquidity May Be Tightening Again - And Markets May Soon Feel It
data.description:
The S&P 500 has been dead money since the end of October, rising by less than 50 bps. For the NASDAQ 100, it has been a bit worse, with the index falling nearly 9% from the October high to the mid-November low, and it still remains more than 2% off those highs.

It could have been worse; consider that Bitcoin has fallen a stunning 29% from that October high through January 23. The range-bound equity market and the big decline in Bitcoin are likely a result of lower liquidity levels, as measured by the Federal Reserve's Reserve Balances, which began declining in the late summer and carried through the fourth quarter.

After cutting T-bill issuance in December and running the Treasury General Account down, essentially adding liquidity to the market, the U.S. Treasury is now increasing T-bill issuance, which could start pushing the TGA up, once again draining liquidity from the market.

Liquidity Drain

Following the conclusion of the debt ceiling in July, the Treasury ramped up T-bill issuance, leading to a more than $500 billion rise in the Treasury General Account. When the TGA declines, it adds reserves back to the Fed's balance sheet, and when the TGA rises, it subtracts reserves from the Fed's balance sheet. In December, the Treasury reduced the size of its Treasury Bill issuance, essentially adding liquidity back into the market, helping reserves to rise off their lows.

What is noticeable, though, is that despite the improvement in liquidity, it wasn't enough; the S&P 500, the NASDAQ 100, and Bitcoin still struggled to advance, essentially moving sideways.

At the December FOMC meeting, the committee recognized the stress building in the overnight funding market due to declining liquidity, prompting the Fed to buy Treasury bills in the open market. However, the current pace is proving insufficient to meaningfully boost reserves; it is likely just enough to keep reserves in a steady state, which means liquidity isn't likely to see a meaningful improvement over the near term. In fact, since the Fed announced its open market operations, the asset side of the balance sheet has increased by roughly $50 billion through January 22.

New Headwinds

However, now that the Treasury is ramping up T-Bill issuance again, the TGA is rising. This risks pushing reserve balances lower, meaning that the Fed's T-bill purchases are only blunting the impact, but not stopping the decline. As of last week, Treasury settlements were $34 billion. The week of January 26 is expected to see just over $80 billion.

Warning Signs

If liquidity conditions tighten again, it will be seen first in the overnight funding market. Most notably through the Secured Overnight Financing Rate. The difference between SOFR and Interest on Reserve Balances is a good indicator of overnight funding conditions, especially when that spread widens.

When liquidity is ample, as in 2023 and 2024, SOFR typically trades well below IORB. When conditions are tight, SOFR will start creeping higher and eventually trade above IORB. The overnight market was very tight in the fourth quarter, and has loosened some in January, thanks in part to the Fed purchases, and the decline in the Treasury general account. If liquidity tightens again, this spread should begin to turn higher.

If the spread widens, it leads to increased use of the Fed's standing repo facility, which allows institutions to obtain liquidity to meet their overnight funding needs. This facility can be accessed twice a day, once in the morning after 8 AM ET and again in the afternoon around 1:30 PM ET.

Reasons Why Stocks Have Held Up Better

One reason why stocks may be holding up better than crypto is that margin levels in the equity market have risen. The same thing happened in 2018, with margin balances remaining high even as reserves fell. It is worth noting that eventually margin balances began to decline alongside reserves.

What is noticeable, though, is that the growth rate of margin has slowed, which, given the relationship between margin growth and the Fed's balance sheet growth, is notable. Stocks are obviously highly correlated to changes in margin balance growth rates.

If Treasury settlements start to take a toll, as they did in the fourth quarter, it should become pretty apparent soon. Last week, suggested it may very well already be happening; this week should provide much greater clarity.
